about: Starter library of ready-to-enable architectural invariants: the 5 structural tenets from https://blog.k10s.dev/im-going-back-to-writing-code-by-hand/ (T1–T5), 2 layering/test-rot invariants (L1–L2), 1 discipline-derived check (D1), and 1 quality-metric check (Q1) — the latter three learned from adopting this gate on real repos + the engineering disciplines it pairs with (classes of decay the size/coupling metrics are blind to). Copy the entries you want into your project's invariants.json and TUNE the thresholds/symbols/paths to your codebase. 'status: ready' = implemented check kind; 'status: planned' = on the roadmap (design §4), template shows the intended shape.

tenets.tenet: T1 — Architecture is yours; the coordinator stays thin
tenets.intent: The central struct/class stays a thin coordinator; per-view/feature state lives in its own type — not one God Object (the k10s Model: 30+ fields, 110-case Update).
tenets.tune: Set `struct` to your coordinator type; `max` to a sane ceiling; keep `ratchet` so it can't grow silently.

tenets.tenet: T2 — Per-view isolation; no central dispatch growth
tenets.intent: No ever-growing central branch on a view/mode identity (the k10s `if currentGVR == ...` / 110-case switch). New views plug in via an interface, not new cases.
tenets.tune: Point `on` at the dispatch discriminant. Ratchet-only (a fixed `max` of ~3 also works).

tenets.tenet: T4 — Typed records; never positional arrays
tenets.intent: Data is typed structs/records, never flattened to arrays accessed by magic integer indices (the k10s `ra[3]` = Alloc fragility that breaks silently when a column moves).
tenets.tune: max 0 is strict; raise + ratchet if a legacy area has unavoidable positional access (or use `// anchor:allow no-positional-rows: <reason>`).

tenets.tenet: T5 — Mutations on the main loop; workers send messages
tenets.intent: Background workers must NOT mutate shared state directly (the k10s data race: goroutines writing Model fields with no mutex). They send messages; the main loop mutates.
engines: ast-grep (TS): a property assignment inside a timer/async callback
tenets.tune: Set `inside` to your async entrypoints (setInterval/setTimeout/queueMicrotask/worker.onmessage); `pattern` to the forbidden mutation. Go is supported via @ast-grep/lang-go using inside_kind: go_statement (the heuristic engine cannot express this).

tenets.tenet: T3 — Explicit scope boundary; fight scope creep
tenets.intent: Stay within the project's declared scope (the k10s drift from a GPU dashboard into a k9s clone). New top-level capabilities require a deliberate scope change.
engines: engine-agnostic: extracts dispatch case-labels, flags any not in allowed
tenets.tune: Set `allowed` to in-scope capability names (flags out-of-scope renames a ratchet misses) and/or `allowedDirs` to in-scope top-level feature dirs (flags a whole new feature area appearing).

tenets.tenet: L1 — Layering: product must not depend on process/working-state
tenets.intent: Product code (src/, test/) must not reach into a lower/sibling layer's working state — e.g. tests reading the build/orchestration scratch dir. Wrong-direction dependencies make the product un-extractable and couple it to ephemeral state (the failure that fused a real repo's test suite to its pipeline's coordination/ tree). Size/coupling metrics never see this.
engines: language-agnostic: counts files under `dirs` that reference `path` in CODE (comment citations are stripped first, so provenance notes don't trip it)
tenets.tune: PER-PROJECT — only forbid a path that is genuinely a DIFFERENT layer your product shouldn't touch. Set `path` to that layer's dir (regex); `dirs` to your product layers. Do NOT forbid a path your product legitimately owns (e.g. on the orchestrator repo itself, coordination/ IS the product domain). Suppress legacy refs with `// anchor:allow product-not-read-process: <reason>`.

tenets.tenet: L2 — No time-bomb tests (tests that can only rot)
tenets.intent: No test pinned to a FROZEN git reference — `git diff <ref>..HEAD`, `--name-only` anti-scope diffs, `git show <sha>`, or a pinned-SHA const. Such a test can only rot: once HEAD moves past that point it fails regardless of product correctness. Round/PR-scoped checks (anti-scope, frozen-surface) belong in a round-aware GATE (see ../install-hook.sh / a pre-merge script), not the permanent suite.
engines: language-agnostic: flags test files (*.test.* / *.spec.*) that BOTH invoke git AND carry a frozen-ref marker (so a product SHA-256 hash test that never touches git is not flagged)
tenets.tune: Universal (no product should pin a frozen ref) — usually adopt as-is. Set `dirs` to your test roots. Suppress a deliberate, maintained case with `// anchor:allow no-time-bomb-tests: <reason>`.

tenets.tenet: D1 — Test coverage floor (the TDD shadow)
tenets.intent: Every source module ships with a test. This is the deterministic shadow of test-driven-development: it can't prove a test was written FIRST, but it enforces TDD's durable outcome — no untested code lands. Matched by base name, layout-agnostic (foo.ts ↔ foo.{test,spec}.* | foo_test.go | test_foo.py | foo_test.py, anywhere in the tree).
engines: language-agnostic: counts source files (.ts/.tsx/.js/.mjs/.cjs/.jsx/.go/.py) under `dirs` with no base-name-matching test; excludes the tests themselves, generated artifacts, and barrel index.* files
tenets.tune: Set `dirs` to your source roots. Keep `ratchet` (no absolute `max`) so today's untested/legacy files are grandfathered and only NEW untested modules block — set `max: 0` only on a greenfield repo you want fully covered. Override the barrel exclusion with `exclude` (regex on the relative path); suppress a deliberate exception with `// anchor:allow require-tests: <reason>`. Pairs with the test-driven-development discipline in `disciplines.md`.

tenets.tenet: Q1 — Bounded function complexity (the principled god-function check)
tenets.intent: No function whose cyclomatic complexity exceeds the limit (≈ 1 + decision points + short-circuit &&/||). A LESS-ARBITRARY signal than raw line count: it flags BRANCHY functions (genuinely hard to follow and test), not merely long-but-flat ones. Cyclomatic > ~10 is the McCabe/NIST anchor. Prefer this over (or alongside) a line-count god-function rule.
engines: ast-grep (Go / TypeScript / JS / Python): per-function decision-node + &&/|| count from the same parse as the god-function check — deterministic, no model cost
tenets.tune: Set `maxComplexity` to your ceiling (10 = McCabe/NIST; 12–15 is a forgiving starter). Ratchet so existing complex functions are grandfathered and only NEW over-complex ones block. A legitimately-branchy-but-cohesive function (a flat dispatch, a parser) is a deliberate `// anchor:allow no-complex-functions: <reason>` — visible, not silent. The number is a convention; the ratchet + suppression are what make it fair. Pairs with / replaces a `max_function_lines` rule.

tenets.tenet: S1 — Bounded dependency surface (supply chain doesn't grow for free)
tenets.intent: The third-party dependency surface must not grow silently. Every added package enlarges the attack + maintenance surface and is a place AI codegen reaches for instead of writing (or reusing) code. A ratchet makes each addition a deliberate, auditable re-baseline rather than a free side effect of 'just install it'. Counts DECLARED deps in a manifest — the size/coupling metrics never see this axis.
engines: deterministic manifest parse (npm package.json; Go go.mod; pip requirements.txt) — no model, offline
tenets.tune: Set `manifest` to your manifest path; `include` to the dependency groups that count toward your surface (default runtime deps + optional; add `devDependencies` to also gate tooling growth). Ratchet so today's deps are grandfathered and only a NEW dependency blocks — bumping the baseline is the auditable record that you chose to add it. go.mod counts direct requires (skips `// indirect`); requirements.txt counts non-comment lines.

tenets.tenet: S2 — No hallucinated / unresolved dependencies (slopsquat guard)
tenets.intent: Every dependency DECLARED in package.json must RESOLVE in the lockfile. A declared-but-unlocked package is the deterministic, OFFLINE fingerprint of a hallucinated or typo'd ('slopsquatted') dependency — a name an AI invented that was never really installed — caught with no registry call and no model. This is the supply-chain failure that a behavioral test and the structural metrics are both blind to.
engines: deterministic: npm package.json vs package-lock.json (lockfile v1 `dependencies` + v2/v3 `packages`) — offline, no model
tenets.tune: Use an absolute `max: 0` (not a ratchet — you never want even one phantom dep). peerDependencies are excluded by default (the consumer resolves them). A deliberately-unlocked dep (a `file:`/`workspace:`/`link:` local package) goes in `allow: ["name"]` — visible in the invariants file, not silent. npm only for now; go.sum / pnpm / yarn lockfiles are a follow-up.

tenets.tenet: M1 — Meta-ratchet: the gate guards itself (no silent relaxation)
tenets.intent: The invariants config + baseline may only move FORWARD (stricter), never backward. Closes the reverse gear in the one-way clutch: an AI-loop fixer (or a hurried human) can make a violation vanish not by fixing the code but by raising a `max`, dropping a rule, downgrading severity, or re-recording baseline.json upward to grandfather more debt — and the relaxed config then passes, silently. This rule counts those relaxations vs a git ref. Because it lives in the invariant set it guards, deleting it is itself a counted relaxation.
engines: deterministic git diff of config vs ref (raised/removed max, removed rule, severity downgrade, raised baseline) — no model, no ast-grep
tenets.tune: Point `invariants`/`baseline` at your config paths (relative to the checked dir) and `against` at the ref to compare to (HEAD for a pre-commit hook comparing the working/staged config to the last commit). Use `max: 0`. A deliberate, REVIEWED loosening (you really do want to raise a threshold) is run with `ARCH_ALLOW_RELAX=1` — the relaxations are still printed to stderr, so the escape hatch is visible, never silent. Pair with fail-closed: together they stop the gate becoming a no-op either by accident (dead engine) or on purpose (relaxed config).

tenets.tenet: TS1 — No new `any` (don't silence the type system)
tenets.intent: The `any` type is the #1 way AI codegen makes a type error disappear without fixing it — it switches OFF the type system locally, so the very guarantee you adopted TypeScript for stops holding. Ratchet `any` usages (the `as any` cast, the `: any` annotation, `<any>`, `any[]`) toward zero: existing ones are grandfathered, NEW ones block. Strong types make illegal states unrepresentable — a guarantee the AI can't violate — which beats adding more runtime checks.
engines: ast-grep (TS/JS), recursive per-file over the whole tree — counts every `any` type node, deterministic, no model
tenets.tune: Ratchet (no absolute `max`) grandfathers today's `any`s and blocks new ones; `max: 0` on a greenfield repo forbids them entirely. A genuinely-unavoidable `any` (an untyped third-party boundary) is a deliberate `// anchor:allow no-new-any: <reason>` on that line — visible, not silent. Set `lang` to `ts` (covers .ts/.tsx/.js/.jsx).

tenets.tenet: TS2 — No non-null assertions (`!`) (don't lie to the compiler)
tenets.intent: The non-null assertion `x!` tells the compiler 'trust me, not null' with no check — a runtime `undefined` waiting to happen, and another favourite AI shortcut to clear a type error. Ratchet `x!` toward zero; prefer a real null check or a type that can't be null.
engines: ast-grep (TS/JS), recursive per-file — counts `non_null_expression` nodes (the `!` operator), not boolean `!`/`!=`
tenets.tune: Ratchet so existing assertions are grandfathered and new ones block. A justified assertion (provably-non-null after a guard the type system can't follow) is a `// anchor:allow no-non-null-assertion: <reason>`.

tenets.intent: `@ts-ignore` and `@ts-nocheck` switch the type checker OFF for a line or a whole file — the bluntest escape hatch, and exactly what an AI reaches for when it can't make the types work. Ratchet toward zero. (Prefer `@ts-expect-error`, which at least FAILS when the underlying error is fixed, so it can't rot silently — it is intentionally NOT matched here.)
engines: ast-grep (TS/JS), recursive per-file — counts comment nodes matching @ts-ignore / @ts-nocheck (not @ts-expect-error)
tenets.tune: Ratchet so existing directives are grandfathered, new ones block. Migrate a real exception to `@ts-expect-error` (self-removing) or suppress with `// anchor:allow no-ts-ignore: <reason>`.
